pages.title: Complexes of homomorphisms
pages.html:
1 Complexes of homomorphisms

1.1

Let \(F^{\bullet}\) and \(G^{\bullet}\) be two graded modules; then one writes:

\[ \tag{1} \operatorname{Hom}^{\bullet}(F^{\bullet}, G^{\bullet}) \]

for the graded module of homomorphisms of graded modules from \(F^{\bullet}\) to \(G^{\bullet}\). Thus one has:

\[ \tag{2} \operatorname{Hom}^{s}(F^{\bullet}, G^{\bullet})=\prod_{k}\operatorname{Hom}(F^{k}, G^{k+s}). \]

Let \(F^{\bullet}\) (resp. \(G^{\bullet}\)) be a complex, and let \(d_{1}\) (resp. \(d_{2}\)) be its differential; then for \(h\in\operatorname{Hom}^{s}(F^{\bullet}, G^{\bullet})\) one sets

\[ \tag{3} d(h)=h\circ d_{1}+(-1)^{s}d_{2}\circ h. \]

One checks trivially that \(d\circ d=0\), hence that \(\operatorname{Hom}^{\bullet}(F^{\bullet}, G^{\bullet})\) equipped with \(d\) is a complex. The cohomology group of this complex is written

\[ \tag{4} \underset{\sim}{\mathrm{H}}^{\bullet}(F^{\bullet}, G^{\bullet}). \]

If \(G^{\bullet}\) is injective in each degree, then

\[ F^{\bullet}\rightsquigarrow\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{\bullet}(F^{\bullet}, G^{\bullet}) \]

is an exact \(\partial\)-functor. Likewise, for arbitrary \(F^{\bullet}\),

\[ G^{\bullet}\rightsquigarrow\underline{\operatorname{H}}^{\bullet}(F^{\bullet}, G^{\bullet}) \]

is an exact \(\delta\)-functor on the category of complexes \(G^{\bullet}\) injective in each degree.

Remark 1.2

The cycles of \(\operatorname{Hom}^{\bullet}(F^{\bullet}, G^{\bullet})\) are the homomorphisms from \(F^{\bullet}\) to \(G^{\bullet}\) that commute or anticommute, according to the degree, with the differentials. The boundaries of \(\operatorname{Hom}^{\bullet}(F^{\bullet}, G^{\bullet})\) are the homomorphisms from \(F^{\bullet}\) to \(G^{\bullet}\) homotopic to zero.

Let \(A\) be a ring and let \(M\) (resp. \(N\)) be an \(A\)-module, \(R(M)\) (resp. \(R(N)\)) an injective resolution of \(M\) (resp. \(N\)); then there exists a canonical isomorphism

\[ \tag{1.3} \underset{\sim}{\mathrm{H}}^{s}(R(M), R(N))\simeq\operatorname{Ext}^{s}(M, N). \]

Indeed, let \(i\colon M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R(M)\) be the canonical augmentation, and let \(h\in\operatorname{Hom}^{s}(R(M), R(N))\); then one writes \(t_{s}\) for the map

\[ h\rightsquigarrow h_{0}\circ i \]

from \(\operatorname{Hom}^{s}(R(M), R(N))\) to \(\operatorname{Hom}(M, R(N)^s)\). The family \(((-1)^{s}t_{s})_{s\geqslant 0}\) defines a homomorphism of (ordinary) complexes

\[ t\colon\operatorname{Hom}^{\bullet}(R(M), R(N))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(M, R(N)), \]

i.e. one has \((dh)_{0}\circ i=(-1) d_{2}\circ h_{0}\circ i.\)

One checks easily that, on passing to cohomology, \(t\) gives an isomorphism. In particular it follows that

\[ \underset{\sim}{\mathrm{H}}^{\bullet}(R(M), R(N)) \]

does not "depend" on the chosen injective resolution \(R(M)\) (resp. \(R(N)\)) of \(M\) (resp. \(N\)).

To every exact sequence of \(A\)-modules

\[ \tag{5} 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M^{\prime}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M^{\prime\prime}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

one associates an exact sequence of injective resolutions

\[ \tag{6} 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R(M^{\prime})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R(M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R(M^{\prime\prime})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0. \]

One checks that the isomorphism (1.3) commutes with the homomorphisms:

\begin{align*} \tag{8} \operatorname{H}^{s}(R(M^{\prime}), R(N))&\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^{s+1}(R(M^{\prime\prime}), R(N)),\\ \tag{9} \operatorname{Ext}^{s}(R(M^{\prime}), R(N))&\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Ext}^{s+1}(R(M^{\prime\prime}), R(N)), \end{align*}

deduced from (6) and (5).

Let \(P\) be a third \(A\)-module, \(R(P)\) an injective resolution of \(P\); then the composition of graded morphisms gives a pairing

\[ \tag{10} \operatorname{Hom}^{i}(R(N), R(M))\times\operatorname{Hom}^{j}(R(M), R(P))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}^{i+j}(R(N), R(P)), \]

which defines a pairing:

\[ \tag{11} \underset{\sim}{\mathrm{H}}^{i}(R(N), R(M))\times\underset{\sim}{\mathrm{H}}^{j}(R(M), R(P))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underset{\sim}{\mathrm{H}}^{i+j}(R(N), R(P)), \]

hence a homomorphism of functors in \(M\):

\[ \tag{1.4} \underset{\sim}{\mathrm{H}}^{i}(R(N), R(M))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{H}^{j}(R(M), R(P)), \operatorname{H}^{i+j}(R(N), R(P))). \]

We shall see that (5.4.) is a homomorphism of \(\delta\)-functors in \(M\). The exact sequences (5) and (6) give a commutative diagram:

\[ \xymatrix{ \operatorname{Hom}^{i}(R(N), R(M^{\prime}))\ar[r]\ar[d] & \operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Hom}^{j}(R(M^{\prime}), R(P)), \operatorname{Hom}^{i+j}(R(N), R(P)))\ar[d]^{\operatorname{Hom}(q, \operatorname{id})}\\ \operatorname{Hom}^{i}(R(N), R(M))\ar[r]\ar[d]^{P} & \operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Hom}^{j}(R(M), R(P)), \operatorname{Hom}^{i+j}(R(N), R(P)))\ar[d]\\ \operatorname{Hom}^{i}(R(N), R(M^{\prime\prime}))\ar[r] & \operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Hom}^{j}(R(M^{\prime\prime}), R(P)), \operatorname{Hom}^{i+j}(R(N), R(P))).} \]

Let \(h\in\operatorname{Hom}^{i}(R(N), R(M^{\prime\prime}))\) (resp. \(g\in\operatorname{Hom}^{j}(R(M^{\prime}), R(P))\)) be a cycle, and let \(h^{\prime}\in\operatorname{Hom}^{i}(R(N), R(M))\) (resp. \(g^{\prime}\in\operatorname{Hom}^{j}(R(M), R(P))\)) be such that \(p(h^{\prime})=h\) (resp. \(q(g^{\prime})=g\)); then to say that (1.4) is a homomorphism of \(\delta\)-functors in \(M\) is to say that

\[ \tag{12} g\circ dh^{\prime}-dg^{\prime}\circ h \]

is a coboundary in \(\operatorname{Hom}^{\bullet}(R(N), R(P))\).

Now one has:

\begin{align*} dh^{\prime} &= h^{\prime}\circ d_{1}+(-1)^{i}d_{2}\circ h^{\prime}\\ dg^{\prime} &= g^{\prime}\circ d_{2}+(-1)^{j}d_{3}\circ g^{\prime} \end{align*}

with the obvious notation. Hence (12) is written:

\begin{align*} g\circ h^{\prime}\circ d_{1} &+ (-1)^{i}g\circ d_{2}\circ h^{\prime}, \\ -g^{\prime}\circ d_{2}\circ h &- (-1)^{j}d_{3}\circ g^{\prime}\circ h. \end{align*}

On the other hand, since \(h\) and \(g\) are cycles one has:

\begin{align*} g\circ d_{2} &= (-1)^{j+1}d_{3}\circ g, \\ d_{2}\circ h &= (-1)^{i+1}h\circ d_{1}, \end{align*}

hence, finally, (12) is written:

\[ d(g\circ h^{\prime}+(-1)^{i}g^{\prime}\circ h), \]

which completes the proof.

(1.3) and (1.4) thus give a homomorphism of \(\delta\)-functors in \(M\):

\[ \tag{1.5} \operatorname{Ext}^{i}(N, M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, P), \operatorname{Ext}^{i+j}(N, P)). \]

blocks.html:
Remark 1.2

The cycles of \(\operatorname{Hom}^{\bullet}(F^{\bullet}, G^{\bullet})\) are the homomorphisms from \(F^{\bullet}\) to \(G^{\bullet}\) that commute or anticommute, according to the degree, with the differentials. Let \(A\) be a ring and let \(M\) (resp. \(N\)) be an \(A\)-module, \(R(M)\) (resp. \(R(N)\)) an injective resolution of \(M\) (resp. \(N\)); then there exists a canonical isomorphism
\[ \tag{1.3} \underset{\sim}{\mathrm{H}}^{s}(R(M), R(N))\simeq\operatorname{Ext}^{s}(M, N). \]

Indeed, let \(i\colon M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R(M)\) be the canonical augmentation, and let \(h\in\operatorname{Hom}^{s}(R(M), R(N))\); then one writes \(t_{s}\) for the map
\[ h\rightsquigarrow h_{0}\circ i \]
from \(\operatorname{Hom}^{s}(R(M), R(N))\) to \(\operatorname{Hom}(M, R(N)^s)\). The family \(((-1)^{s}t_{s})_{s\geqslant 0}\) defines a homomorphism of (ordinary) complexes
\[ t\colon\operatorname{Hom}^{\bullet}(R(M), R(N))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(M, R(N)), \]
i.e. one has \((dh)_{0}\circ i=(-1) d_{2}\circ h_{0}\circ i.\)
One checks easily that, on passing to cohomology, \(t\) gives an isomorphism. In particular it follows that
\[ \underset{\sim}{\mathrm{H}}^{\bullet}(R(M), R(N)) \]
does not "depend" on the chosen injective resolution \(R(M)\) (resp. \(R(N)\)) of \(M\) (resp. \(N\)).
To every exact sequence of \(A\)-modules

\[ \tag{5} 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M^{\prime}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M^{\prime\prime}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]
one associates an exact sequence of injective resolutions

\[ \tag{6} 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R(M^{\prime})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R(M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R(M^{\prime\prime})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0. \]
One checks that the isomorphism (1.3) commutes with the homomorphisms:

\begin{align*} \tag{8} \operatorname{H}^{s}(R(M^{\prime}), R(N))&\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^{s+1}(R(M^{\prime\prime}), R(N)),\\ \tag{9} \operatorname{Ext}^{s}(R(M^{\prime}), R(N))&\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Ext}^{s+1}(R(M^{\prime\prime}), R(N)), \end{align*}
deduced from (6) and (5).
Let \(P\) be a third \(A\)-module, \(R(P)\) an injective resolution of \(P\); then the composition of graded morphisms gives a pairing
\[ \tag{10} \operatorname{Hom}^{i}(R(N), R(M))\times\operatorname{Hom}^{j}(R(M), R(P))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}^{i+j}(R(N), R(P)), \]
which defines a pairing:

\[ \tag{11} \underset{\sim}{\mathrm{H}}^{i}(R(N), R(M))\times\underset{\sim}{\mathrm{H}}^{j}(R(M), R(P))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underset{\sim}{\mathrm{H}}^{i+j}(R(N), R(P)), \]
hence a homomorphism of functors in \(M\):

\[ \tag{1.4} \underset{\sim}{\mathrm{H}}^{i}(R(N), R(M))\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{H}^{j}(R(M), R(P)), \operatorname{H}^{i+j}(R(N), R(P))). \]
We shall see that (5.4.) is a homomorphism of \(\delta\)-functors in \(M\). The exact sequences (5) and (6) give a commutative diagram:

\[ \xymatrix{ \operatorname{Hom}^{i}(R(N), R(M^{\prime}))\ar[r]\ar[d] & \operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Hom}^{j}(R(M^{\prime}), R(P)), \operatorname{Hom}^{i+j}(R(N), R(P)))\ar[d]^{\operatorname{Hom}(q, \operatorname{id})}\\ \operatorname{Hom}^{i}(R(N), R(M))\ar[r]\ar[d]^{P} & \operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Hom}^{j}(R(M), R(P)), \operatorname{Hom}^{i+j}(R(N), R(P)))\ar[d]\\ \operatorname{Hom}^{i}(R(N), R(M^{\prime\prime}))\ar[r] & \operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Hom}^{j}(R(M^{\prime\prime}), R(P)), \operatorname{Hom}^{i+j}(R(N), R(P))).} \]

Let \(h\in\operatorname{Hom}^{i}(R(N), R(M^{\prime\prime}))\) (resp. \(g\in\operatorname{Hom}^{j}(R(M^{\prime}), R(P))\)) be a cycle, and let \(h^{\prime}\in\operatorname{Hom}^{i}(R(N), R(M))\) (resp. \(g^{\prime}\in\operatorname{Hom}^{j}(R(M), R(P))\)) be such that \(p(h^{\prime})=h\) (resp. \(q(g^{\prime})=g\)); then to say that (1.4) is a homomorphism of \(\delta\)-functors in \(M\) is to say that
\[ \tag{12} g\circ dh^{\prime}-dg^{\prime}\circ h \]
is a coboundary in \(\operatorname{Hom}^{\bullet}(R(N), R(P))\).
Now one has:
\begin{align*} dh^{\prime} &= h^{\prime}\circ d_{1}+(-1)^{i}d_{2}\circ h^{\prime}\\ dg^{\prime} &= g^{\prime}\circ d_{2}+(-1)^{j}d_{3}\circ g^{\prime} \end{align*}
with the obvious notation. Hence (12) is written:
\begin{align*} g\circ h^{\prime}\circ d_{1} &+ (-1)^{i}g\circ d_{2}\circ h^{\prime}, \\ -g^{\prime}\circ d_{2}\circ h &- (-1)^{j}d_{3}\circ g^{\prime}\circ h. \end{align*}
On the other hand, since \(h\) and \(g\) are cycles one has:
\begin{align*} g\circ d_{2} &= (-1)^{j+1}d_{3}\circ g, \\ d_{2}\circ h &= (-1)^{i+1}h\circ d_{1}, \end{align*}
hence, finally, (12) is written:
\[ d(g\circ h^{\prime}+(-1)^{i}g^{\prime}\circ h), \]
which completes the proof.
(1.3) and (1.4) thus give a homomorphism of \(\delta\)-functors in \(M\):
\[ \tag{1.5} \operatorname{Ext}^{i}(N, M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, P), \operatorname{Ext}^{i+j}(N, P)). \]

pages.title: The local duality theorem for a regular local ring
pages.html:
2 The local duality theorem for a regular local ring

Let \(A\) be a regular local ring of dimension \(r\), let \(\mathfrak{m}\) be the maximal ideal of \(A\) and let \(M\) be a \(A\)-module of finite type. We set \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)=\operatorname{H}^{i}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) (so that \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)=\varinjlim\operatorname{Ext}^{i}(A/\mathfrak{m}^{k}, M)\)). We have seen (IV 5.4) that \(I=\operatorname{H}^{r}(A)\) is a dualizing module for \(A\); let us denote by \(D\) the associated dualizing functor. In (1.5) let us set \(N=A/\mathfrak{m}^{k}\), \(P=A\); then we obtain a homomorphism of \(\delta\)-functors in \(M\)

\[ \tag{13} \varphi_{k}\colon\operatorname{Ext}^{i}(A/\mathfrak{m}^{k}, M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Ext}^{r-i}(M, A), \operatorname{Ext}^{r}(A/\mathfrak{m}^{k}, A)). \]

Passing to the inductive limit over \(k\), we find a homomorphism of \(\delta\)-functors

\[ \tag{14} \varphi\colon\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} D(\operatorname{Ext}^{r-i}(M, A)). \]
Theorem 2.1 (Local duality theorem)

The preceding functorial homomorphism \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.

Proof▾

If \(i>r\), the second member of (14) is trivially zero, and the first member is zero because \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)=\varinjlim_{k}\operatorname{Ext}^{i}(A/\mathfrak{m}^{k}, M)\), and this holds for each \(\operatorname{Ext}^{i}(A/\mathfrak{m}^{k}, M)\) (syzygy theorem).

If \(i=r\), by what precedes, the two functors in \(M\), \(\operatorname{H}^{r}(M)\) and \(D(\operatorname{Hom}(M, A))\) are right exact; since \(A\) is noetherian, and \(M\) of finite type, it suffices to verify the isomorphism for \(M=A\), which is immediate.

To show that \(\varphi\) is a functorial isomorphism, it now suffices, proceeding by descending induction with respect to \(i\), to remark that every module of finite type admits a finite presentation, and that for \(i<r\) the two members of (14) are zero if \(M\) is free of finite type. This is evident for the second member, and since \(\operatorname{H}^{i}\) commutes with finite sums it suffices, as regards the first, to show that \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(A)=0\) for \(i<r\). Now this results, since \(\mathrm{prof}.\, A=r\), from (III 3.4).

Q.E.D.

pages.title: Application to the structure of the \(\operatorname{H}^i(M)\)
pages.html:
3 Application to the structure of \(\operatorname{H}^i(M)\)

Theorem 3.1

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring, \(D\) a dualizing functor for \(A\), \(M\) an \(A\)-module of finite type \(\neq 0\), and of dimension \(n\); then we have:

  1. \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)=0\) if \(i<0\) or if \(i>n\).

  2. \(D(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M))\;\widehat{\hbox{}}\) is a module of finite type over \(\widehat{A}\), of dimension \(\leqslant i\).

  3. \(\operatorname{H}^{n}(M)\neq 0\), and if \(A=\widehat{A}\), \(D(\operatorname{H}^{n}(M))\) is of dimension \(n\) and \(\operatorname{Ass}(D(\operatorname{H}^{n}(M)))=\{ \mathfrak{p}\in\operatorname{Ass}(M) \mid \mathrm{dim}.\, A/\mathfrak{p}=n\}\).

Proof▾

Let \(I\) be the dualizing module associated with \(D\). We know that \(\widehat{I}\) is a dualizing module for \(\widehat{A}\). On the other hand, we have:

  1. \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)\;\widehat{\hbox{}}=\operatorname{H}^{i}(\widehat{M})\),

  2. \(D(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M))\;\widehat{\hbox{}}=\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{H}^{i}(\widehat{M}), \widehat{I})\) and

  3. \(\mathrm{dim}.\,\widehat{M}=\mathrm{dim}.\, M\),

hence we may assume \(A\) complete. Now, by a theorem of Cohen, every complete local ring is a quotient of a regular local ring. To reduce to this case, we need the following lemma:

Lemma 3.2

Let \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)) be a ringed space, \(X^{\prime}\) (resp. \(Y^{\prime}\)) a closed subspace of \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)), and \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a morphism of ringed spaces such that \(f^{-1}(Y^{\prime})=X^{\prime}\). Let \(F\) be an \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module and denote by \(A\) (resp. \(B\)) the ring \(\Gamma(\mathscr{O}_X)\) (resp. \(\Gamma(\mathscr{O}_{Y})\)) and by \(\overline{f}\colon B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) the ring homomorphism corresponding to \(f\). There exists a spectral sequence of \(B\)-modules, with initial term

\[ \tag{15} E_{2}^{p, q}=\operatorname{H}_{Y^{\prime}}^{p}(Y, \operatorname{R}^{q}f_{\ast}(F)), \]

abutting to the \(B\)-module \(\operatorname{H}_{X^{\prime}}^{\bullet}(X, F)_{[\overline{f}]}\).

Proof▾

Let \(\mathscr{O}_{Y, Y^{\prime}}\) be the sheaf \({\mathscr{O}_{Y}}{|Y^{\prime}}\) extended by \(0\) outside \(Y^{\prime}\) (see Exp. I). We have an isomorphism of \(B\)-modules:

\[ \tag{16} \operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{O}_{Y, Y^{\prime}}, f_{\ast}(F))\simeq\operatorname{Hom}(f^{\ast}(\mathscr{O}_{Y, Y^{\prime}}), F)_{[\overline{f}]}. \]

Now, we have:

\[ \tag{17} f^{\ast}(\mathscr{O}_{Y, Y^{\prime}})=\mathscr{O}_{X, X^{\prime}}, \]

and moreover if \(G\) is an injective \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module, then \(f_{\ast}(G)\) is an injective \(\mathscr{O}_{Y}\)-Module, at least if \(f\) is flat, a case to which one can easily reduce by replacing \(\mathscr{O}_X\) etc. by the constant sheaves of rings \(\mathbf{Z}\). Hence the spectral sequence of the composite functor

\[ F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{O}_{Y, Y^{\prime}}, f_{\ast}(F)), \]

with initial term

\[ E_{2}^{p, q}=\operatorname{Ext}^{p}(Y;\mathscr{O}_{Y, Y^{\prime}}, \operatorname{R}^{q}f_{\ast}(F)), \]

abuts, taking into account (16) and (17), to:

\[ \operatorname{Ext}^{\bullet}(X;\mathscr{O}_{X, X^{\prime}}, F)_{[\overline{f}]}. \]

The lemma then follows from (I 2.3.

Q.E.D.

Let now \(f\colon B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) be a surjective homomorphism of local rings. Let

\[ f\colon\operatorname{Spec}(A)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Spec}(B) \]

be the corresponding morphism of affine schemes. Set \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) (resp. \(X^{\prime}=\{\mathfrak{m}_{A}\}\)), \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}.(B)\) (resp. \(Y^{\prime}=\{\mathfrak{m}_{B}\}\)), and let \(M\) be an \(A\)-module and \(\widetilde{M}\) the corresponding \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Since \(\operatorname{R}^{q}f_{\ast}(\widetilde{M})=0\) for \(q>0\), the spectral sequence (15) degenerates, and by (3.2) we obtain an isomorphism of \(B\) modules:

\[ \tag{18} \operatorname{H}_{\{\mathfrak{m}_{B}\} }^{n}(Y, f_{\ast}(\widetilde{M}))\simeq\operatorname{H}_{\{\mathfrak{m}_{A}\} }^{n}(X, \widetilde{M})_{[f]}, \]

hence an isomorphism of \(B\)-modules:

\[ \tag{19} \operatorname{H}_{\mathfrak{m}_{B}}^{n}(M_{[f]})\simeq\operatorname{H}_{\mathfrak{m}_{A}}^{n}(M)_{[f]}. \]

On the other hand, if \(D_{A}\) (resp. \(D_{B}\)) is the dualizing functor for \(A\) (resp. \(B\)), we have:

\[ \tag{20} D_{A}(M)_{[f]}\simeq D_{B}(M_{[f]}). \]

Finally, since we have an isomorphism of rings

\[ \tag{21} B/\mathrm{Ann}.\, M_{[f]}\simeq A/\mathrm{Ann}.\, M, \]

we see that the change of base ring under consideration changes nothing. Suppose therefore that \(A\) is regular of dimension \(r\).

By (2.1) we have:

\[ \tag{22} D(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M))=\operatorname{Ext}^{r-i}(M, A). \]

We are going to prove the equivalence of the following properties:

  1. (a)

    \(\mathrm{dim}.\, \operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A)\leqslant r-j\);

  2. (b)

    for every \(\mathfrak{p}\in X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) such that \(\mathrm{dim}.\, A_{\mathfrak{p}}<j\), we have \(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A)_{\mathfrak{p}}=0\);

  3. (c)

    \(\operatorname{codim}(\operatorname{Supp}(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A)), X)\geqslant j\).

To prove (a) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (b), let \(\mathfrak{p}\in X\), \(\mathrm{dim}.\, A_\mathfrak{p}<j\), then \(\dim A/\mathfrak{p}>r-j\), hence by (a) \(\operatorname{Ann}(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A))\not\subset\mathfrak{p}\), which entails \(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A)_{\mathfrak{p}}=0\). Let \(\mathfrak{p}\in\operatorname{Supp}(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A))\), then \(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A)_{\mathfrak{p}}\neq 0\), hence by (b) \(\dim A_\mathfrak{p}\geqslant j\). Hence \(\operatorname{codim}(\operatorname{Supp}(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A)), X)=\inf\{ \dim A_\mathfrak{p}\mid \mathfrak{p}\in\operatorname{Supp}(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A))\} \geqslant j\), that is to say (b) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (c). Finally (c) implies (a) trivially.

Let us now prove the theorem.

  1. Let \(x=(x_{1},\dots, x_{r})\) be a system of parameters for \(A\) such that \(x_{i}\in\mathrm{Ann}.\, M\) for \(i=1,\dots, r-n\). Let \(K^{\bullet}((\underline{x}^{k}), M)\) be the Koszul complex. One sees easily that the map \(K^{i}((x^{k}), M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K^{i}((x^{k^{\prime}}), M)\) for \(k<k^{\prime}\) is zero, if \(i>n\). It follows that \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)=\varinjlim.\operatorname{H}^{i}((x^{k}), M)=0\) if \(i>n\). On the other hand, it is trivial that \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)=0\) if \(i<0\), hence (i) is proved.

  2. Since \(A\) is regular, \(\dim A_{\mathfrak{p}}<j\) entails \(\dim\operatorname{gl}{.}A_{\mathfrak{p}}<j\), hence \(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A)_{\mathfrak{p}}=\operatorname{Ext}_{A_{\mathfrak{p}}}^{j}(M_{\mathfrak{p}}, A_{\mathfrak{p}})=0\), hence we have proved (b) and consequently (a). (ii) then follows from (22) and from (a).

  3. There exists a \(\mathfrak{p}\in\operatorname{Supp}(M)\) such that \(\dim A_{\mathfrak{p}}=r-n\) and such that \(\operatorname{Supp}(M_{\mathfrak{p}})=\{ \mathfrak{m} A_{\mathfrak{p}}\}\). Since \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\) is regular if \(A\) is, one finds \(\operatorname{prof} A_{\mathfrak{p}}=r-n\), hence

    \[ \tag{23} \operatorname{Ext}_{A}^{r-n}(M, A)_{\mathfrak{p}}=\operatorname{Ext}_{A_{\mathfrak{p}}}^{r-n}(M_{\mathfrak{p}}, A_{\mathfrak{p}})\neq 0. \]

    This implies, taking into account (22), that on the one hand:

    \[ \operatorname{H}^{n}(M)\neq 0, \]

    and on the other hand

    \[ \mathrm{dim}.\, D(\operatorname{H}^{n}(M))\geqslant n, \]

    hence by (ii)

    \[ \mathrm{dim}.\, D(\operatorname{H}^{n}(M))=n. \]

    Let now \(Y=\operatorname{Supp}(M)\). By (i) we know that \(D(\operatorname{H}^{n}(M^{\prime}))=\operatorname{Ext}^{r-n}(M^{\prime}, A)\) is a functor in \(M^{\prime}\), left exact, on the category \((\mathbf{C}_{Y})^{\circ}\). Hence there exists an \(A\)-module \(H\) and an isomorphism of functors in \(M^{\prime}\):

    \[ \operatorname{Ext}^{r-n}(M^{\prime}, A)=\operatorname{Hom}(M^{\prime}, H). \]

    Let \(Y_{i}\), \(i=1,\dots, k\) be the irreducible components of \(Y\) of maximal dimension. We shall see that the assertion \(\operatorname{Ext}^{r-n}(M^{\prime}, A)\neq 0\) is equivalent to the assertion: there exists an \(i\) such that \(\operatorname{Supp} M^{\prime}\supset Y_{i}\). Indeed, if \(\operatorname{Supp} M^{\prime}\supset Y_{i}\) then \(\mathrm{dim}.\, M^{\prime}=n\), hence \(\operatorname{Ext}^{r-n}(M^{\prime}, A)\neq 0\).

    If \(\operatorname{Supp} M^{\prime}\not\supset Y_{i}\) for all \(i=1,\dots, k\), then \(\mathrm{dim}.\, M^{\prime}<n\)

    \[ D(\operatorname{H}^{n}(M^{\prime}))=\operatorname{Ext}^{r-n}(M^{\prime}, A)=0. \]

    Since \(\operatorname{Ass}(\operatorname{Ext}^{r-n}(M, A))=\operatorname{Supp} M\cap\operatorname{Ass} H\) we see that the last assertion of (iii) follows from the following lemma:

Lemma 3.3

Let \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and let \(Y\) be a closed part of \(X\), \(T\colon (\mathbf{C}_{Y})^{\circ}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\) a left exact functor, and \(Y_{i}\), \(i=1,\dots, k\) a family of irreducible components of \(Y\) such that the assertion: \(T(M)=0\) is equivalent to the assertion: \(\forall i\;\operatorname{Supp} M\not\supset Y_{i}\). Then \(T\) is representable by a module \(H\) such that \(\operatorname{Ass}.H=\bigcup_{i=1}^{k}\{y_{i}\}\), where \(y_{i}\) is the generic point of \(Y_{i}\), \(i=1,\dots, k\).

Proof▾

Let \(y\in Y\); one constructs an \(A\)-module \(M(y)\) such that \(\operatorname{Supp}(M(y))=\overline{\{ y\} }\). Suppose that \(y\neq y_{i}\) for all \(i=1,\dots, k\), then \(Y_{i}\not\subset\operatorname{Supp}(M(y))\) for all \(i=1,\dots, k\), hence \(T(M)(y))=0\). It follows:

\[ \operatorname{Ass}(T(M(y)))=\operatorname{Supp}(M(y))\cap\operatorname{Ass} H=\emptyset, \]

hence \(y\not\in\operatorname{Ass} H\). If \(y=y_{i}\), then \(Y_{i}\subset\operatorname{Supp}(M(y))\), hence \(T(M(y))\neq 0\), hence:

\[ \operatorname{Ass}(T(M(y)))=\operatorname{Supp}(M)\cap\operatorname{Ass} H\neq\emptyset. \]

By the first part of the proof, this implies \(y\in\operatorname{Ass} H\), whence the lemma, Q.E.D.

Q.E.D.
Q.E.D.
Example 3.4

Let \(A\) be a noetherian ring, let \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and \(Y\) a closed part of \(X\), such that \(X-Y\) is affine; then for every irreducible component \(Y_{\alpha}\) of \(Y\) we have \(\operatorname{codim}.(Y_{\alpha}, X)\leqslant 1\).

Indeed, let us regard \(X\) as a prescheme over \(X\). Let \(y_{\alpha}\in Y_{\alpha}\) be a generic point, and consider the morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Y_{\alpha}})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). The affine scheme obtained by extension of the base scheme of \(X\) to \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Y_{\alpha}})\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Y_{\alpha}})\).

By (EGA I 3.2.7) we see that if \(y_{0}\) is the unique closed point of \(Y_{0}=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Y_{\alpha}})\), then \(Y_{0}- y_{0}\) is affine. By (EGA III 1.3.1) we find:

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(Y_{0}- y_{0}, \mathscr{O}_{Y_{0}})=0\quad\textrm{ if }i>0, \]

hence by (I.2.11)

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Y_{\alpha}})=\operatorname{H}_{\{ y_{0}\} }^{i}(Y_{0}, \mathscr{O}_{Y_{0}})=0\quad\textrm{ if }i\geqslant 2. \]

Taking into account (5.7.) it comes:

\[ \mathrm{dim}.\, \mathscr{O}_{X, y_{\alpha}}\leqslant 1, \]

hence \(\mathrm{codim}.\, (Y_{\alpha}, X)=\underset{y\in Y_{\alpha}}{\inf}\mathrm{dim}.\, \mathscr{O}_{X, y}\leqslant 1\),Q.E.D.

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring, \(\mathfrak{m}\) the maximal ideal and \(M\) an \(A\)-module of finite type. Suppose that \(A\) is a quotient of a regular local ring. Set \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and for every \(x\in X\), \(\mathfrak{m}_{x}=\mathfrak{m} A_{x}\).

Proposition 3.5

The following two conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)\) is of finite length,

  2. \(\forall x\in X-\{ \mathfrak{m}\}, \, \operatorname{H}_{\mathfrak{m}_{x}}^{i-\dim\overline{\{ x\} }}(M_{x})=0\).

Proof▾

Taking into account 3.3) we may assume \(A\) regular. By formula (1.3) we have:

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(M)=D(\operatorname{Ext}^{r-i}(M, A)), \]

where \(r=\dim.A\). By (IV 4.7), a) is equivalent to:

\[ \tag{24} \operatorname{Ext}^{n-i}(M, A)\textrm{ is of finite length.} \]

Now (24) is equivalent to:

\[ \tag{25} \forall x\in X-\{ \mathfrak{m}\}, \textrm{ we~have }\operatorname{Ext}^{r-i}(M, A)_{x}=0. \]

On the other hand \(A_{x}\) is regular of dimension \(r-\dim\overline{\{ x\} }\), hence by (2.1)

\[ \operatorname{H}_{\mathfrak{m}_{x}}^{i-\dim\overline{\{ x\} }}(M_{x})\!=\!D(\operatorname{Ext}_{A_{x}}^{(r-\dim\overline{\{ x\} })-(i-\dim\overline{\{ x\} })}(M_{x}, A_{x}))\!=\!D(\operatorname{Ext}_{A_{x}}^{r-i}(M_{x}, A_{x})).\tag*{(26)} \]

Since \(M\) is of finite type we have:

\[ \operatorname{Ext}_{A}^{r-i}(M, A)_{x}=\operatorname{Ext}_{A_{x}}^{r-i}(M_{x}, A_{x}) \]

whence the proposition.

Q.E.D.
Corollary 3.6

In order that \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)\) be of finite length for \(i\leqslant n\), it is necessary and sufficient that

\[ \mathrm{prof}.\, M_{x}>n-\dim\overline{\{ x\} } \]

for every \(x\in X-\{ \mathfrak{m}\}\).

Proof▾

Follows from (3.5) and from (III 3.1).

Q.E.D.

blocks.html: 3 Application to the structure of \(\operatorname{H}^i(M)\)

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.1

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring, \(D\) a dualizing functor for \(A\), \(M\) an \(A\)-module of finite type \(\neq 0\), and of dimension \(n\); then we have:

  1. \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)=0\) if \(i<0\) or if \(i>n\).

  2. \(D(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M))\;\widehat{\hbox{}}\) is a module of finite type over \(\widehat{A}\), of dimension \(\leqslant i\).

  3. \(\operatorname{H}^{n}(M)\neq 0\), and if \(A=\widehat{A}\), \(D(\operatorname{H}^{n}(M))\) is of dimension \(n\) and \(\operatorname{Ass}(D(\operatorname{H}^{n}(M)))=\{ \mathfrak{p}\in\operatorname{Ass}(M) \mid \mathrm{dim}.\, A/\mathfrak{p}=n\}\).

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

Let \(I\) be the dualizing module associated with \(D\). We know that \(\widehat{I}\) is a dualizing module for \(\widehat{A}\). On the other hand, we have:

  1. \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)\;\widehat{\hbox{}}=\operatorname{H}^{i}(\widehat{M})\),

  2. \(D(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M))\;\widehat{\hbox{}}=\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{H}^{i}(\widehat{M}), \widehat{I})\) and

  3. \(\mathrm{dim}.\,\widehat{M}=\mathrm{dim}.\, M\),

hence we may assume \(A\) complete. Now, by a theorem of Cohen, every complete local ring is a quotient of a regular local ring. To reduce to this case, we need the following lemma:

Lemma 3.2

Let \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)) be a ringed space, \(X^{\prime}\) (resp. \(Y^{\prime}\)) a closed subspace of \(X\) (resp. \(Y\)), and \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) a morphism of ringed spaces such that \(f^{-1}(Y^{\prime})=X^{\prime}\). Let \(F\) be an \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module and denote by \(A\) (resp. \(B\)) the ring \(\Gamma(\mathscr{O}_X)\) (resp. \(\Gamma(\mathscr{O}_{Y})\)) and by \(\overline{f}\colon B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) the ring homomorphism corresponding to \(f\). There exists a spectral sequence of \(B\)-modules, with initial term

\[ \tag{15} E_{2}^{p, q}=\operatorname{H}_{Y^{\prime}}^{p}(Y, \operatorname{R}^{q}f_{\ast}(F)), \]

abutting to the \(B\)-module \(\operatorname{H}_{X^{\prime}}^{\bullet}(X, F)_{[\overline{f}]}\).

Proof▾

Let \(\mathscr{O}_{Y, Y^{\prime}}\) be the sheaf \({\mathscr{O}_{Y}}{|Y^{\prime}}\) extended by \(0\) outside \(Y^{\prime}\) (see Exp. I). We have an isomorphism of \(B\)-modules:

\[ \tag{16} \operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{O}_{Y, Y^{\prime}}, f_{\ast}(F))\simeq\operatorname{Hom}(f^{\ast}(\mathscr{O}_{Y, Y^{\prime}}), F)_{[\overline{f}]}. \]

Now, we have:

\[ \tag{17} f^{\ast}(\mathscr{O}_{Y, Y^{\prime}})=\mathscr{O}_{X, X^{\prime}}, \]

and moreover if \(G\) is an injective \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module, then \(f_{\ast}(G)\) is an injective \(\mathscr{O}_{Y}\)-Module, at least if \(f\) is flat, a case to which one can easily reduce by replacing \(\mathscr{O}_X\) etc. by the constant sheaves of rings \(\mathbf{Z}\). Hence the spectral sequence of the composite functor

\[ F\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(\mathscr{O}_{Y, Y^{\prime}}, f_{\ast}(F)), \]

with initial term

\[ E_{2}^{p, q}=\operatorname{Ext}^{p}(Y;\mathscr{O}_{Y, Y^{\prime}}, \operatorname{R}^{q}f_{\ast}(F)), \]

abuts, taking into account (16) and (17), to:

\[ \operatorname{Ext}^{\bullet}(X;\mathscr{O}_{X, X^{\prime}}, F)_{[\overline{f}]}. \]

The lemma then follows from (I 2.3.

Q.E.D.

Let now \(f\colon B\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} A\) be a surjective homomorphism of local rings. Let

\[ f\colon\operatorname{Spec}(A)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{Spec}(B) \]

be the corresponding morphism of affine schemes. Set \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) (resp. \(X^{\prime}=\{\mathfrak{m}_{A}\}\)), \(Y=\operatorname{Spec}.(B)\) (resp. \(Y^{\prime}=\{\mathfrak{m}_{B}\}\)), and let \(M\) be an \(A\)-module and \(\widetilde{M}\) the corresponding \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Since \(\operatorname{R}^{q}f_{\ast}(\widetilde{M})=0\) for \(q>0\), the spectral sequence (15) degenerates, and by (3.2) we obtain an isomorphism of \(B\) modules:

\[ \tag{18} \operatorname{H}_{\{\mathfrak{m}_{B}\} }^{n}(Y, f_{\ast}(\widetilde{M}))\simeq\operatorname{H}_{\{\mathfrak{m}_{A}\} }^{n}(X, \widetilde{M})_{[f]}, \]

hence an isomorphism of \(B\)-modules:

\[ \tag{19} \operatorname{H}_{\mathfrak{m}_{B}}^{n}(M_{[f]})\simeq\operatorname{H}_{\mathfrak{m}_{A}}^{n}(M)_{[f]}. \]

On the other hand, if \(D_{A}\) (resp. \(D_{B}\)) is the dualizing functor for \(A\) (resp. \(B\)), we have:

\[ \tag{20} D_{A}(M)_{[f]}\simeq D_{B}(M_{[f]}). \]

Finally, since we have an isomorphism of rings

\[ \tag{21} B/\mathrm{Ann}.\, M_{[f]}\simeq A/\mathrm{Ann}.\, M, \]

we see that the change of base ring under consideration changes nothing. Suppose therefore that \(A\) is regular of dimension \(r\).

By (2.1) we have:

\[ \tag{22} D(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M))=\operatorname{Ext}^{r-i}(M, A). \]

We are going to prove the equivalence of the following properties:

  1. (a)

    \(\mathrm{dim}.\, \operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A)\leqslant r-j\);

  2. (b)

    for every \(\mathfrak{p}\in X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) such that \(\mathrm{dim}.\, A_{\mathfrak{p}}<j\), we have \(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A)_{\mathfrak{p}}=0\);

  3. (c)

    \(\operatorname{codim}(\operatorname{Supp}(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A)), X)\geqslant j\).

To prove (a) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (b), let \(\mathfrak{p}\in X\), \(\mathrm{dim}.\, A_\mathfrak{p}<j\), then \(\dim A/\mathfrak{p}>r-j\), hence by (a) \(\operatorname{Ann}(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A))\not\subset\mathfrak{p}\), which entails \(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A)_{\mathfrak{p}}=0\). Let \(\mathfrak{p}\in\operatorname{Supp}(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A))\), then \(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A)_{\mathfrak{p}}\neq 0\), hence by (b) \(\dim A_\mathfrak{p}\geqslant j\). Hence \(\operatorname{codim}(\operatorname{Supp}(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A)), X)=\inf\{ \dim A_\mathfrak{p}\mid \mathfrak{p}\in\operatorname{Supp}(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A))\} \geqslant j\), that is to say (b) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (c). Finally (c) implies (a) trivially.

Let us now prove the theorem.

  1. Let \(x=(x_{1},\dots, x_{r})\) be a system of parameters for \(A\) such that \(x_{i}\in\mathrm{Ann}.\, M\) for \(i=1,\dots, r-n\). Let \(K^{\bullet}((\underline{x}^{k}), M)\) be the Koszul complex. One sees easily that the map \(K^{i}((x^{k}), M)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K^{i}((x^{k^{\prime}}), M)\) for \(k<k^{\prime}\) is zero, if \(i>n\). It follows that \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)=\varinjlim.\operatorname{H}^{i}((x^{k}), M)=0\) if \(i>n\). On the other hand, it is trivial that \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)=0\) if \(i<0\), hence (i) is proved.

  2. Since \(A\) is regular, \(\dim A_{\mathfrak{p}}<j\) entails \(\dim\operatorname{gl}{.}A_{\mathfrak{p}}<j\), hence \(\operatorname{Ext}^{j}(M, A)_{\mathfrak{p}}=\operatorname{Ext}_{A_{\mathfrak{p}}}^{j}(M_{\mathfrak{p}}, A_{\mathfrak{p}})=0\), hence we have proved (b) and consequently (a). (ii) then follows from (22) and from (a).

  3. There exists a \(\mathfrak{p}\in\operatorname{Supp}(M)\) such that \(\dim A_{\mathfrak{p}}=r-n\) and such that \(\operatorname{Supp}(M_{\mathfrak{p}})=\{ \mathfrak{m} A_{\mathfrak{p}}\}\). Since \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\) is regular if \(A\) is, one finds \(\operatorname{prof} A_{\mathfrak{p}}=r-n\), hence

    \[ \tag{23} \operatorname{Ext}_{A}^{r-n}(M, A)_{\mathfrak{p}}=\operatorname{Ext}_{A_{\mathfrak{p}}}^{r-n}(M_{\mathfrak{p}}, A_{\mathfrak{p}})\neq 0. \]

    This implies, taking into account (22), that on the one hand:

    \[ \operatorname{H}^{n}(M)\neq 0, \]

    and on the other hand

    \[ \mathrm{dim}.\, D(\operatorname{H}^{n}(M))\geqslant n, \]

    hence by (ii)

    \[ \mathrm{dim}.\, D(\operatorname{H}^{n}(M))=n. \]

    Let now \(Y=\operatorname{Supp}(M)\). By (i) we know that \(D(\operatorname{H}^{n}(M^{\prime}))=\operatorname{Ext}^{r-n}(M^{\prime}, A)\) is a functor in \(M^{\prime}\), left exact, on the category \((\mathbf{C}_{Y})^{\circ}\). Hence there exists an \(A\)-module \(H\) and an isomorphism of functors in \(M^{\prime}\):

    \[ \operatorname{Ext}^{r-n}(M^{\prime}, A)=\operatorname{Hom}(M^{\prime}, H). \]

    Let \(Y_{i}\), \(i=1,\dots, k\) be the irreducible components of \(Y\) of maximal dimension. We shall see that the assertion \(\operatorname{Ext}^{r-n}(M^{\prime}, A)\neq 0\) is equivalent to the assertion: there exists an \(i\) such that \(\operatorname{Supp} M^{\prime}\supset Y_{i}\). Indeed, if \(\operatorname{Supp} M^{\prime}\supset Y_{i}\) then \(\mathrm{dim}.\, M^{\prime}=n\), hence \(\operatorname{Ext}^{r-n}(M^{\prime}, A)\neq 0\).

    If \(\operatorname{Supp} M^{\prime}\not\supset Y_{i}\) for all \(i=1,\dots, k\), then \(\mathrm{dim}.\, M^{\prime}<n\)

    \[ D(\operatorname{H}^{n}(M^{\prime}))=\operatorname{Ext}^{r-n}(M^{\prime}, A)=0. \]

    Since \(\operatorname{Ass}(\operatorname{Ext}^{r-n}(M, A))=\operatorname{Supp} M\cap\operatorname{Ass} H\) we see that the last assertion of (iii) follows from the following lemma:

Lemma 3.3

Let \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and let \(Y\) be a closed part of \(X\), \(T\colon (\mathbf{C}_{Y})^{\circ}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\) a left exact functor, and \(Y_{i}\), \(i=1,\dots, k\) a family of irreducible components of \(Y\) such that the assertion: \(T(M)=0\) is equivalent to the assertion: \(\forall i\;\operatorname{Supp} M\not\supset Y_{i}\). Then \(T\) is representable by a module \(H\) such that \(\operatorname{Ass}.H=\bigcup_{i=1}^{k}\{y_{i}\}\), where \(y_{i}\) is the generic point of \(Y_{i}\), \(i=1,\dots, k\).

Proof▾

Let \(y\in Y\); one constructs an \(A\)-module \(M(y)\) such that \(\operatorname{Supp}(M(y))=\overline{\{ y\} }\). Suppose that \(y\neq y_{i}\) for all \(i=1,\dots, k\), then \(Y_{i}\not\subset\operatorname{Supp}(M(y))\) for all \(i=1,\dots, k\), hence \(T(M)(y))=0\). It follows:

\[ \operatorname{Ass}(T(M(y)))=\operatorname{Supp}(M(y))\cap\operatorname{Ass} H=\emptyset, \]

hence \(y\not\in\operatorname{Ass} H\). If \(y=y_{i}\), then \(Y_{i}\subset\operatorname{Supp}(M(y))\), hence \(T(M(y))\neq 0\), hence:

\[ \operatorname{Ass}(T(M(y)))=\operatorname{Supp}(M)\cap\operatorname{Ass} H\neq\emptyset. \]

By the first part of the proof, this implies \(y\in\operatorname{Ass} H\), whence the lemma, Q.E.D.

Q.E.D.
Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 3.4

Let \(A\) be a noetherian ring, let \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and \(Y\) a closed part of \(X\), such that \(X-Y\) is affine; then for every irreducible component \(Y_{\alpha}\) of \(Y\) we have \(\operatorname{codim}.(Y_{\alpha}, X)\leqslant 1\).

Indeed, let us regard \(X\) as a prescheme over \(X\). Let \(y_{\alpha}\in Y_{\alpha}\) be a generic point, and consider the morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Y_{\alpha}})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\). The affine scheme obtained by extension of the base scheme of \(X\) to \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Y_{\alpha}})\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Y_{\alpha}})\).

By (EGA I 3.2.7) we see that if \(y_{0}\) is the unique closed point of \(Y_{0}=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Y_{\alpha}})\), then \(Y_{0}- y_{0}\) is affine. By (EGA III 1.3.1) we find:

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(Y_{0}- y_{0}, \mathscr{O}_{Y_{0}})=0\quad\textrm{ if }i>0, \]

hence by (I.2.11)

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(\mathscr{O}_{X, Y_{\alpha}})=\operatorname{H}_{\{ y_{0}\} }^{i}(Y_{0}, \mathscr{O}_{Y_{0}})=0\quad\textrm{ if }i\geqslant 2. \]

Taking into account (5.7.) it comes:

\[ \mathrm{dim}.\, \mathscr{O}_{X, y_{\alpha}}\leqslant 1, \]

hence \(\mathrm{codim}.\, (Y_{\alpha}, X)=\underset{y\in Y_{\alpha}}{\inf}\mathrm{dim}.\, \mathscr{O}_{X, y}\leqslant 1\),Q.E.D.

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring, \(\mathfrak{m}\) the maximal ideal and \(M\) an \(A\)-module of finite type. Suppose that \(A\) is a quotient of a regular local ring. Set \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and for every \(x\in X\), \(\mathfrak{m}_{x}=\mathfrak{m} A_{x}\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.5

The following two conditions are equivalent:

  1. \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)\) is of finite length,

  2. \(\forall x\in X-\{ \mathfrak{m}\}, \, \operatorname{H}_{\mathfrak{m}_{x}}^{i-\dim\overline{\{ x\} }}(M_{x})=0\).

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

Taking into account 3.3) we may assume \(A\) regular. By formula (1.3) we have:

\[ \operatorname{H}^{i}(M)=D(\operatorname{Ext}^{r-i}(M, A)), \]

where \(r=\dim.A\). By (IV 4.7), a) is equivalent to:

\[ \tag{24} \operatorname{Ext}^{n-i}(M, A)\textrm{ is of finite length.} \]

Now (24) is equivalent to:

\[ \tag{25} \forall x\in X-\{ \mathfrak{m}\}, \textrm{ we~have }\operatorname{Ext}^{r-i}(M, A)_{x}=0. \]

On the other hand \(A_{x}\) is regular of dimension \(r-\dim\overline{\{ x\} }\), hence by (2.1)

\[ \operatorname{H}_{\mathfrak{m}_{x}}^{i-\dim\overline{\{ x\} }}(M_{x})\!=\!D(\operatorname{Ext}_{A_{x}}^{(r-\dim\overline{\{ x\} })-(i-\dim\overline{\{ x\} })}(M_{x}, A_{x}))\!=\!D(\operatorname{Ext}_{A_{x}}^{r-i}(M_{x}, A_{x})).\tag*{(26)} \]

Since \(M\) is of finite type we have:

\[ \operatorname{Ext}_{A}^{r-i}(M, A)_{x}=\operatorname{Ext}_{A_{x}}^{r-i}(M_{x}, A_{x}) \]

whence the proposition.

Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.6

In order that \(\operatorname{H}^{i}(M)\) be of finite length for \(i\leqslant n\), it is necessary and sufficient that

\[ \mathrm{prof}.\, M_{x}>n-\dim\overline{\{ x\} } \]

for every \(x\in X-\{ \mathfrak{m}\}\).

blocks.title: Proof
blocks.html:
Proof▾

Follows from (3.5) and from (III 3.1).

Q.E.D.
